chunks.text: What if an agent colony could not be fooled the same way twice? After a failed action, the next proposal should be measurably harder to pass. This is Codomyrmex.

context.subtitle: The colony that gets harder to fool

chunks.text: A modular agentic ecosystem of one hundred thirty Python modules, governed by a Colony Control Plane that learns from every action. Agents earn authority. Failures tighten the gate. The colony remembers.

scenes.title: A demo is a value, not a recording

chunks.text: Conventional agent frameworks treat the module ecosystem as a warehouse: any agent can reach for any tool at any time, with no memory of past failures. Codomyrmex models the collective as a colony, where agents earn trust, roles adapt, and the gate tightens on repeated failure.

bullets: Agents are not fungible workers — they are actors with earned trust profiles.
Failures deposit pheromone signals that make the next proposal at that location harder.
Five roles, six signal types, eight subsystems — all governed by a closed feedback loop.

chunks.text: The ColonyKernel is the hub class. It owns the lifecycle of all eight subsystems and exposes a four-method public API: propose, record, status, tick.

The actuation gate renders its verdict through a weighted linear combination of four scores: budget headroom, risk pressure, trust tier, and proposal completeness. Hard overrides refuse SANDBOX roles, low trust, and CRITICAL falsification findings before scoring even begins.

Stigmergy: agents do not negotiate with each other. They deposit signals into a shared pheromone field. The gate reads the field when evaluating the next proposal at that location. Coordination scales as O of n, not O of n squared.

scenes.title: What you are seeing, right now

chunks.text: This video was compiled by DemoCreate from a single declarative file describing Codomyrmex. The code typed itself in. The narration is a real neural voice running fully local. Every frame is rendered, not recorded.

bullets: One hundred thirty top-level modules, each self-contained with its own tests and RASP documentation.
Five hundred ninety three runtime MCP tools exposed for Claude, Gemini, GPT, and any Model Context Protocol client.
Zero-mock policy: all thirty five thousand tests use real data and real computations — no unittest.mock, ever.

chunks.text: The colony kernel runs a continuous loop: pressure generates proposals, proposals pass or are blocked by the gate, surviving actions produce consequences, consequences are written to memory, memory drives role reassignment, and roles reshape the pressure distribution. The loop repeats. The colony learns.

Agents do not start with permissions. They start as SANDBOX. Clean outcomes deposit SUCCESS signals, trust rises, and the role adapter deterministically promotes them through REPAIR ANT, MEMORY ANT, DISPATCHER, and finally GUARD ANT. Failures reverse the trajectory.

bullets: SANDBOX — all proposals refused, no matter the score.
MEMORY_ANT — read and annotate, no destructive actions.
DISPATCHER — coordinate other agents, propose plans.
GUARD_ANT — full authority, review pruning candidates, enforce colony policy.

chunks.text: The pheromone field carries six typed signal types. Each has its own decay rate. FAILURE signals persist longer than SUCCESS, making the colony cautious where things have gone wrong. HUMAN PRIORITY overrides everything. NEED signals attract agents. DEPENDENCY traces the module graph. RISK marks unverified paths.

Before the gate evaluates any proposal, the falsification worker runs deterministic adversarial checks. It attempts to invalidate the proposal — missing tests, no rollback plan, unverified assumptions, scope creep. Only proposals that survive adversarial scrutiny reach the gate.

Under deterministic clean outcomes, trust converges. Each success deposits a signal, the trust score drifts upward, and the role adapter promotes the agent. Under repeated failures, the score drops, the role narrows, and the gate refuses more proposals. The colony adapts its pressure profile to the problem surface.

And it is real. One hundred thirty modules, five hundred ninety three MCP tools, thirty five thousand tests, thirty seven CI workflows, and one thousand two hundred markdown docs — all zero-mock, all real, all tested.
One command verifies the entire colony: doctor checks every module, every MCP tool, every test. Zero-mock means zero shortcuts. Real verification, every time.

chunks.text: The colony that gets harder to fool. One hundred thirty modules. Five roles. Six signals. Eight subsystems. One loop: pressure, proposal, gate, action, consequence, memory, role, pressure. This video was compiled by DemoCreate from one declarative file. Edit it, re-render, and never record a take again.
